title: Rigid heddle cotton napkin set
subtitle: Six dinner napkins woven in 8/2 cotton on one warp at 12 epi
excerpt: A set of six dinner napkins woven from one long warp on a rigid heddle loom. 8/2 cotton warp and weft at 12 epi on a 12-dent reed. Each napkin finishes at approximately 35 x 35 cm after hemstitching and wet-finishing. The warp plan accounts for take-up, shrinkage, and loom waste across all six napkins.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from standard rigid-heddle plain-weave cotton practice. Warp-calculation methods follow Hooper, Hand-Loom Weaving (1910), public domain, Internet Archive. Hemstitch finishing is documented in standard weaving manuals including the guild tradition recorded in Mary E. Black, The Key to Weaving (1945), cited as technical reference; written around, not quoted.

glossaryTerms.definition: The density of the warp measured as ends per inch (epi) or threads per centimetre. For plain weave in 8/2 cotton, a sett of 12 epi produces a balanced cloth where warp and weft are equally visible on the surface.

Ends per inch: the count of warp threads in one inch of width. On a rigid heddle loom, EPI is set by the reed fitted to the loom. A 12-dent reed gives 12 epi when one thread occupies each slot and each hole.

The reduction in warp length that occurs as the weft crimps over and under the warp threads during weaving. Take-up is typically 5-10% of the woven length and must be added to the warp length calculation before measuring out the warp.

The warp length consumed by tying the warp to the front and back apron rods. It cannot be woven. A typical rigid heddle loom uses 40-60 cm of total loom waste, split between the front and back tie-on.

A finishing stitch worked on the loom while the cloth is still under tension. The needle works a figure-of-eight around groups of warp ends, catching the last few picks of the weaving. When the cloth is cut off and the warp tails trimmed, the hemstitch holds the weft picks in place and forms a decorative drawn-thread border.

content.text: Six dinner napkins from one warp on a rigid heddle loom. The napkins are woven in plain weave using 8/2 cotton at
12 epi
on a 12-dent reed. The finished
sett

produces a balanced cloth where warp and weft are equally visible, with good body and drape for table use. Each napkin finishes at approximately 35 x 35 cm after hemstitching and wet-finishing. The warp is 240 cm long to allow for
take-up
, shrinkage, and
loom waste
across all six napkins. This is an effective batch project: once the loom is dressed, the weaving is repetitive and the whole set can come off one continuous warp.
Warp planning

Work out the warp length before setting up the warping board. Six napkins at 35 cm finished length each equals 210 cm of finished weaving. Add 10% for take-up (21 cm) to get 231 cm of loom length. Add 40-60 cm for loom waste (front and back tie-on). Add 3 cm per napkin for the hemstitch allowance at each end of each napkin (6 napkins x 2 ends x 3 cm = 36 cm). The total warp length is approximately 237-257 cm; wind to 260 cm to allow a comfortable margin.

Warp width: a finished napkin of 35 cm plus 5% draw-in allowance gives a working warp of approximately 37 cm. At 12 epi that is 444 ends rounded down to 444. On a 12-dent reed, 444 ends gives 37 cm of warp width. Check the loom's maximum weaving width before cutting yarn; most rigid heddle looms sold as 45 cm or wider will accommodate this warp.

Shrinkage: 8/2 cotton at 12 epi typically shrinks 5-8% in length and 3-5% in width on the first wash. The warp calculation above has built in enough margin that the napkins should finish close to 35 x 35 cm. Weave a short sample before the first napkin (12-15 picks), wet-finish the sample, and measure the shrinkage if precision is important.
The draft

definition.title: Plain weave on rigid heddle, 12 epi

content.text: The draft represents the two sheds on a rigid heddle loom. Shaft 1 shows hole threads (raised when the heddle is lifted); shaft 2 shows slot threads (raised when the heddle is pushed down). Alternating the two sheds in sequence produces a balanced plain weave. At 12 epi the cloth will be dense enough for table use but still flexible enough to fold and stack flat.
Warping and threading

Fit the 12-dent reed to the loom. Set the warping board pegs 260 cm apart. Wind 444 ends of 8/2 cotton at even tension, making the threading cross at the near peg with each circuit. Tie the cross securely before removing the warp from the board.

Thread the rigid heddle by alternating slot and hole: first thread in a slot, second through the next hole, across all 444 ends. Keep the cross tied until threading is done. The cross stops tangles and helps you hold the slot-hole order across the full width.

Tie the warp onto the front and back apron rods in groups of 20-24 ends (about 5 cm of warp width per group). Adjust each group to equal tension before tying the bow knot. Test tension by pressing the flat of your hand lightly across the warp; all ends should spring back with the same resistance. Uneven tension between groups shows as wavy fell lines during weaving and corrugated cloth in the finished piece.
Weaving the napkins

Wind the 8/2 cotton weft onto the stick shuttle. The same cotton used for the warp works as the weft for a plain white or natural cloth. If weaving striped or colour-blocked napkins, wind a separate shuttle for each weft colour.

Weave 10-12 picks of scrap cotton to spread the warp evenly. Check the selvedges after the first few picks; the warp should fill the full 37 cm width without bunching at the centre or gapping at the edges.
Before weaving, work a
hemstitch
along the fell line to lock the first picks in place.

Thread the blunt darning needle with weft yarn. Slide it under 4 warp ends, back over those ends and under 2 picks, then out through the cloth. Step 4 ends along and repeat to the far edge. Each group of 4 ends produces one stitch. At the cut-off stage, trimming the warp tails close to the stitching gives a clean edge on each napkin.

Weave the first napkin to 39 cm on the loom (to allow for take-up and shrinkage to a 35 cm finished length). Beat consistently: aim for 12 picks per inch (same as the warp sett) for a balanced cloth. A firm, parallel beat keeps the napkin square; a varying beat introduces distortion that wet-finishing may not correct.

At the end of the first napkin, work the hemstitch on the final row in the same way as the opening hemstitch. Then weave 6-8 picks of scrap cotton as a spacer before starting the second napkin. The scrap cotton marks the cut line between napkins and keeps the weaving from unravelling while you are handling the piece.

Open the hemstitch on the second napkin, weave 39 cm, hemstitch the end, add a spacer. Repeat for all six napkins. Advance the warp every 8-10 cm to keep the fell line close to the reed and maintain consistent beat pressure.
Finishing

Cut the napkins apart at the scrap-cotton spacers, leaving 1-2 cm of warp tail beyond each hemstitch edge. Trim the warp tails to 1 cm and fold them back behind the hemstitch; press or dab with a small amount of washable fabric glue to keep them flat if needed, or leave them to tuck in during the first wash.

Machine-wash on a gentle cotton cycle at 40 C or hand-wash in warm water with a gentle soap. Cotton napkins can tolerate regular machine washing once the first wet-finish has settled the cloth. Press with a warm iron while slightly damp to crisp the surface and confirm the napkins are square. Fold and stack.

items.problem: The napkin is narrower in the middle than at the start and end
items.cause: The weft is being pulled too taut before beating, drawing the selvedges in
items.fix: Leave a gentle arc of weft thread in the shed before beating each pick. The weft needs just enough slack to curve over and under 444 warp ends without pulling the edges in. Leave the arc about 1 cm above the fell before pressing the heddle home.

items.problem: The finished napkins are noticeably rectangular rather than square
items.cause: The warp was wound to 39 cm but the beat was uneven, or the hemstitched sections consumed more or less warp than planned
items.fix: Measure the on-loom length of each napkin between hemstitched sections rather than relying on pick count alone. A soft tape measure held parallel to the selvedge gives a reliable check. If the first napkin measured accurately and came out too short or too long after washing, adjust the loom length for the remaining five.

items.problem: Loops of weft appear at the selvedge edges on alternating picks
items.cause: The shuttle did not pass completely through the shed, leaving a loop of weft hanging outside the selvedge thread
items.fix: After each pass, check that the shuttle has cleared the last warp thread on the far selvedge before beating. Open the shed fully before throwing; a half-open shed forces the shuttle to ride over warp threads and creates loops.
